title: Isaiah 62
subtitle: You Will Be Called by a New Name

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “Your New Name: Hephzibah and Beulah”

heb.paragraph: "You will be called by a new name that the mouth of the LORD will bestow. You will be called Hephzibah (ḥēfeṣ = my delight is in her), and your land Beulah (married)" (vv.2, 4). "For Zion’s sake I will not keep silent; for Jerusalem’s sake I will not remain quiet, till her vindication shines out like the dawn" (v.1). The renaming: not “Deserted” but “My Delight”; not “Desolate” but “Married.” "As a young man marries a young woman, so will your Builder marry you; as a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so will your God rejoice over you" (v.5). God as bridegroom: not just tolerating but REJOICING.

notes.note: "For Zion’s sake I will not keep silent." God refuses silence. The speaking is relentless: no quiet until the vindication blazes.
"You will be called Hephzibah — My Delight Is in Her. Your land, Beulah — Married." The renaming from shame to delight, from abandonment to marriage. Identity reconstructed by divine speech.

"As a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so will your God rejoice over you." The emotional centre: God REJOICES. Not merely accepts. Rejoices. The strongest statement of divine joy over his people in the OT.

refs.note: The promise of a new name known only to the recipient develops Isaiah's theme of renaming as transformation.
Writing God's name and the new Jerusalem's name on the overcomer continues Isaiah's renaming theology.
Hosea's betrothal imagery parallels Isaiah's 'Beulah' (married) reversal of abandonment.

hist.context: The new names — Hephzibah ('my delight is in her') and Beulah ('married') — reverse Azubah ('forsaken') and Shemamah ('desolate'). In ancient thought, naming expressed essence; renaming signaled ontological change. The bridal metaphor portrays restoration as wedding celebration, with YHWH as the rejoicing bridegroom.

sections.header: Verses 6–12 — “The Watchmen on Your Walls Will Never Be Silent”

heb.paragraph: "I have posted watchmen (shomərîm) on your walls, Jerusalem; they will never be silent day or night. You who call on the LORD, give yourselves no rest, and give him no rest till he establishes Jerusalem and makes her the praise of the earth" (vv.6–7). Relentless prayer: give God no rest! "The LORD has sworn by his right hand and by his mighty arm: Never again will I give your grain as food for your enemies" (v.8). "Pass through, pass through the gates! Prepare the way for the people. Build up, build up the highway! Remove the stones. Raise a banner for the nations" (v.10). The highway-theology reaches its climax: the road is built, the stones removed, the banner raised.

notes.note: "I have posted watchmen who will never be silent. Give HIM no rest till he establishes Jerusalem." The command to exhaust God with prayer. The watchmen are intercessors, not soldiers.
"Pass through the gates! Build up the highway! Raise a banner for the nations!" The highway of 11:16, 35:8, 40:3 reaches its final iteration. The road is ready; the nations are summoned.
"See, your Saviour comes! His reward is with him. They will be called the Holy People, the Redeemed of the LORD." The new names multiply: Holy People, Redeemed, Sought After, City No Longer Deserted.

refs.note: The watchmen on Jerusalem's walls find counterpart in the angels at the new Jerusalem's gates.
The command to pray for Jerusalem's peace resonates with Isaiah's watchmen who give God no rest until he establishes Zion.
The disciples' question about restoring the kingdom reflects ongoing expectation rooted in Isaiah's restoration vision.

hist.context: The watchmen who never keep silent day or night are likely prophetic figures tasked with intercession. 'Giving God no rest' is bold anthropomorphism: human prayer is portrayed as holding God to his promises. The highway preparation and banner-raising recall processional imagery — the exiles' return is envisioned as triumphant parade.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVNo longer will they call you Deserted, or name your land Desolate. But you will be called Hephzibah, and your land Beulah; for the Lord will take delight in you, and your land will be married.ESVYou shall no more be termed Forsaken, and your land shall no more be termed Desolate, but you shall be called My Delight Is in Her, and your land Married; for the LORD delights in you, and your land shall be married.NIVI have posted watchmen on your walls, Jerusalem; they will never be silent day or night. You who call on the Lord, give yourselves no rest,ESVOn your walls, O Jerusalem, I have set watchmen; all the day and all the night they shall never be silent. You who put the LORD in remembrance, take no rest,

rec.text: ""For Zion’s sake I will not keep silent." God refuses silence. The speaking is relentless: no quiet until the vindication blazes."

rows.label: Never Silent — 62:6–12
rows.text: "Watchmen who will NEVER BE SILENT." "Give HIM no rest!" Highway built. Banner raised. "The REDEEMED of the LORD."

hebtext.note: "You will be called by a new name that the mouth of the LORD will bestow. You will be called Hephzibah (ḥēfeṣ = my delight is in her), and your land Beulah (married)" (vv.2, 4). "For Zion’s sake I will not keep silent; for Jerusalem’s sake I will not remain quiet, till her vindication shines out like the dawn" (v.1). The renaming: not “Deserted” but “My Delight”; not “Desolate” but “Married.” "As a young man marries a young woman, so will your Builder marry you; as a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so will your God rejoice over you" (v.5). God as bridegroom: not just tolerating but REJOICING.

"I have posted watchmen (shomərîm) on your walls, Jerusalem; they will never be silent day or night. You who call on the LORD, give yourselves no rest, and give him no rest till he establishes Jerusalem and makes her the praise of the earth" (vv.6–7). Relentless prayer: give God no rest! "The LORD has sworn by his right hand and by his mighty arm: Never again will I give your grain as food for your enemies" (v.8). "Pass through, pass through the gates! Prepare the way for the people. Build up, build up the highway! Remove the stones. Raise a banner for the nations" (v.10). The highway-theology reaches its climax: the road is built, the stones removed, the banner raised.

themes.note: Covenant and Mercy peak at 9: Hephzibah, Beulah — renamed by God. The bridegroom REJOICES. Watchmen who never stop praying. The highway complete.

coaching.tip: The renaming — from 'Deserted' to Hephzibah ('My Delight Is in Her'), from 'Desolate' to Beulah ('Married') — is covenant restoration expressed as intimacy. Names carry destiny in Hebrew thought. God doesn't just forgive Jerusalem; he re-identifies her. The new names replace the shame-names that exile imposed. Identity transformation, not just behavioral adjustment, is what redemption looks like.

'I have posted watchmen on your walls, Jerusalem; they will never be silent day or night' (v. 6). These watchmen are intercessors — their job is to remind God of his promises and give him 'no rest' until he acts (v. 7). Isaiah sanctions persistent, even aggressive, prayer. The watchmen don't inform God of things he's forgotten; they hold him to things he's promised. Prayer is covenant enforcement.
